title: Adversarial and property tests

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Adversarial and property tests; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Adversarial and property tests
sources.claim: Adversarial tests target empty, singleton, duplicate, ordered, reverse, extreme, disconnected, cyclic, and scale cases derived from algorithm assumptions.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Adversarial tests target empty, singleton, duplicate, ordered, reverse, extreme, disconnected, cyclic, and scale cases derived from algorithm assumptions.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A binary-search suite includes empty, absent, first, last, duplicates, and maximum-size sorted input. Your goal is to create a reusable test matrix that distinguishes algorithm correctness from a few passing examples. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Greedy, backtracking and dynamic programming; Directed and weighted graph algorithms; Sequence and range problem patterns; Correctness and counterexamples; Components and cycle detection; Tabulation and state reconstruction. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: Adversarial tests target empty, singleton, duplicate, ordered, reverse, extreme, disconnected, cyclic, and scale cases derived from algorithm assumptions. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: Concept evidence is shared across C, C++, Java, and Python, but C pointer/lifetime safety, C++ iterator/ownership rules, Java generics/equality, and Python mutability/hashability are separate implementation facets that require evidence.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Random tests need trustworthy oracles, overflow and recursion limits are language-specific, and hidden tests should not reward hacks. The draft therefore rejects: Large random inputs alone cover every important algorithm boundary.

includes: Test empty, singleton, duplicate, sorted, reverse, disconnected, cyclic, malformed and size-boundary inputs as applicable.
Create a reusable test matrix that distinguishes algorithm correctness from a few passing examples.

Concept evidence is shared across C, C++, Java, and Python, but C pointer/lifetime safety, C++ iterator/ownership rules, Java generics/equality, and Python mutability/hashability are separate implementation facets that require evidence.
excludes: Random tests need trustworthy oracles, overflow and recursion limits are language-specific, and hidden tests should not reward hacks.

outcomes: Create a reusable test matrix that distinguishes algorithm correctness from a few passing examples.

examples.title: Adversarial and property tests: language-neutral trace
examples.situation: A binary-search suite includes empty, absent, first, last, duplicates, and maximum-size sorted input.
walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: A binary-search suite includes empty, absent, first, last, duplicates, and maximum-size sorted input.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Derive adversarial cases from invariants and failure modes, then add randomized comparison to a trusted oracle.
examples.result: The conceptual result follows because Derive adversarial cases from invariants and failure modes, then add randomized comparison to a trusted oracle.

examples.title: Adversarial and property tests: implementation transfer
examples.situation: Concept evidence is shared across C, C++, Java, and Python, but C pointer/lifetime safety, C++ iterator/ownership rules, Java generics/equality, and Python mutability/hashability are separate implementation facets that require evidence.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Keep the same specification and invariant across all four languages.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: Concept evidence is shared across C, C++, Java, and Python, but C pointer/lifetime safety, C++ iterator/ownership rules, Java generics/equality, and Python mutability/hashability are separate implementation facets that require evidence.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: Random tests need trustworthy oracles, overflow and recursion limits are language-specific, and hidden tests should not reward hacks.
examples.result: Concept mastery transfers, while implementation evidence remains language-specific.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Large random inputs alone cover every important algorithm boundary.
misconceptions.correction: Derive adversarial cases from invariants and failure modes, then add randomized comparison to a trusted oracle.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use an invariant or counterexample to explain why this DSA belief fails: Large random inputs alone cover every important algorithm boundary.

analogy.example: A carefully labeled physical arrangement can introduce Adversarial and property tests, while the canonical invariant and operations remain the actual algorithmic model.
usefulFor: Introducing structure, order, connectivity, or state transition before formal notation
limitations: A physical arrangement does not reproduce asymptotic cost, aliasing, numeric limits, recursion depth, or the four languages' implementation rules.
Random tests need trustworthy oracles, overflow and recursion limits are language-specific, and hidden tests should not reward hacks.

Keep this boundary in view: Random tests need trustworthy oracles, overflow and recursion limits are language-specific, and hidden tests should not reward hacks.
